figures.name: Wu Ding
figures.significance: Greatest Shang king; oracle bone inscriptions from his reign are the oldest surviving Chinese writing

name: Zhou Dynasty
note: Late Zhou (Warring States) produced Confucius, Laozi, Mencius, Han Fei — the foundational philosophical schools that would define all subsequent Chinese governance

figures.name: Confucius (Kong Qiu)
figures.significance: Defined the moral and political philosophy that governed China for 2500 years

figures.name: Sun Tzu
figures.significance: Author of The Art of War

figures.name: Laozi
figures.significance: Founder of Taoism; author of the Tao Te Ching

name: Qin Dynasty
note: State philosophy was Legalism — entirely different from Confucianism. Qin's brutal efficiency unified China but was ideologically rejected by every subsequent dynasty

figures.name: Qin Shi Huang
figures.significance: First Emperor of unified China; built the Great Wall and Terracotta Army; his title Huangdi was used by every subsequent emperor

figures.name: Li Si
figures.significance: Grand Councillor; architect of Qin Legalism and the first unified Chinese bureaucracy

name: Han Dynasty
note: Established Confucianism as state ideology and the civil examination system — the template all later Chinese dynasties followed

figures.name: Liu Bang (Emperor Gaozu)
figures.significance: Rose from peasant to emperor; the Han ethnic identity is named after his dynasty

figures.name: Emperor Wu of Han
figures.significance: Established Confucianism as state ideology; opened the Silk Road

figures.name: Sima Qian
figures.significance: Father of Chinese historiography; his Records of the Grand Historian defined the writing of Chinese history for 2000 years

name: Sui Dynasty
note: Buddhism was a major state religion alongside Confucianism; Sui rulers were devout Buddhists

figures.name: Emperor Wen (Yang Jian)
figures.significance: Reunified China after 370 years of division; built the Grand Canal

name: Tang Dynasty
note: Tang royal family had partial Xianbei ancestry but identified as Han; cosmopolitan — Buddhism, Taoism, Nestorian Christianity, and Islam all coexisted; Confucian exam system perfected

policies.name: Huichang Suppression of Buddhism
policies.description: Emperor Wuzong destroyed monasteries and forced monks to return to secular life. Buddhism restored under the next emperor. Tang ideology remained Confucian.

figures.name: Emperor Taizong (Li Shimin)
figures.significance: Considered China greatest emperor; his Zhenguan era is the model of virtuous governance

figures.name: Wu Zetian
figures.significance: The only woman in Chinese history to hold the title of Empress Regnant

figures.name: Li Bai
figures.significance: Greatest Tang poet; defines the image of Tang cosmopolitan culture

name: Liao Dynasty (Khitan)
note: Dual administration: Khitan customs for nomads, Chinese Confucian system for Han subjects; Buddhism served as a bridge between nomadic and sedentary cultures

figures.name: Abaoji (Emperor Taizu of Liao)
figures.significance: United the Khitan tribes; created the Khitan script; invented the dual-administration system

name: Song Dynasty
note: Neo-Confucianism (synthesizing Buddhist metaphysics with Confucian ethics) became the dominant intellectual tradition

figures.name: Zhao Kuangyin (Emperor Taizu)
figures.significance: Former general who peacefully demilitarized the warlords; built China most sophisticated civilian bureaucracy

figures.name: Su Shi (Su Dongpo)
figures.significance: Poet, painter, calligrapher, and statesman; the Renaissance Man of the Song dynasty

name: Jin Dynasty (Jurchen)
note: Jurchen rulers resisted Sinicization; maintained Jurchen script and shamanistic traditions alongside Buddhism; used a dual admin system similar to Liao

figures.name: Wanyan Aguda (Emperor Taizu of Jin)
figures.significance: United the Jurchen tribes; created the Jurchen script to resist cultural assimilation

name: Yuan Dynasty
note: Kublai Khan adopted Tibetan Buddhism (Vajrayana) as state religion and Phags-pa Lama as spiritual authority; deliberately avoided Sinicization — Chinese barred from top posts; Mongol identity maintained as ruling class

figures.name: Kublai Khan
figures.significance: Founded the Yuan dynasty; first non-Han ruler to control all of China

name: Ming Dynasty
note: Founded as an explicit rejection of Mongol rule; strong Han nativist and Confucian identity; the 'most Chinese' of dynasties

figures.name: Zhu Yuanzhang (Hongwu Emperor)
figures.significance: Rose from orphaned peasant to emperor; expelled the Mongols

figures.name: Zheng He
figures.significance: Led seven maritime expeditions reaching Arabia and East Africa; commanded fleets that dwarfed anything in Europe

figures.name: Yongle Emperor
figures.significance: Built the Forbidden City; commissioned the Yongle Encyclopedia

name: Qing Dynasty (Manchu)
note: Manchu rulers governed Han majority but maintained strict Manchu identity: forbidden intermarriage, maintained bannerman system, Manchu language in court. Vajrayana Buddhism was a deliberate tool to legitimize rule over Mongols and Tibetans. Projected Confucian face outward while preserving Manchu identity internally — a masterclass in dual identity governance

policies.name: Queue Order (Hair Cutting Decree)
policies.description: All Han men required to adopt the Manchu queue hairstyle. Policy of ethnic dominance, not ideological shift.

figures.name: Nurhaci
figures.significance: United the Jurchen tribes; created the Eight Banner military system

figures.name: Kangxi Emperor
figures.significance: Longest-reigning Chinese emperor (61 years); consolidated Qing rule

figures.name: Qianlong Emperor
figures.significance: Greatest territorial extent; commissioned the Siku Quanshu — the largest library in history

figures.name: Puyi
figures.significance: Last Emperor of China; the final chapter of 2000 years of imperial rule

name: Goguryeo
note: One of the Three Kingdoms of Korea; at its peak controlled northern Manchuria and the Korean peninsula north of the Han River. Repelled three massive Sui Chinese invasions (612–614) — the failure of those campaigns contributed to the Sui Dynasty's collapse. Finally destroyed by a Tang–Silla alliance in 668. Goguryeo identity is claimed by both modern Korea and China (as part of Gaogouli history).

figures.name: Gwanggaeto the Great
figures.significance: Expanded Goguryeo to its greatest extent — 64 walled cities and 1,400 villages conquered; his stele is the primary source for early Korean history; called 'Great King who Broadly Extended Territory'

figures.name: Eulji Mundeok
figures.significance: Defeated Sui Emperor Yang's million-man invasion (612) using strategic retreat and the Battle of Salsu; one of the greatest defensive victories in East Asian history; the Sui Dynasty never recovered

name: Unified Silla
note: Silla allied with the Tang Dynasty to defeat Baekje and Goguryeo, then successfully expelled Tang forces to unify the peninsula south of the Taedong River.

name: Goryeo Dynasty
note: Founded by Wang Geon; successfully resisted multiple Mongol (Yuan) invasions, eventually becoming a vassal-son-in-law state of the Yuan. Known for the Tripitaka Koreana and Goryeo celadon.

name: Three Kingdoms of Korea
note: Confederation of three competing kingdoms: Goguryeo (north), Baekje (southwest), and Silla (southeast). All three eventually unified under Silla after Tang-Silla alliance eliminated rivals.

name: Feudal Japan
note: The Kamakura, Muromachi, and Sengoku periods. Decentralized feudal system with competing daimyo (lords) and regional warlords. Ends with the unification by Oda Nobunaga and Toyotomi Hideyoshi, preparing ground for Tokugawa shogunate.

name: Japanese-Occupied Korea
note: Japanese colonial period following the Japan-Korea Treaty of 1910. Direct imperial administration, suppression of Korean language and culture, and economic exploitation. Ended with Japan's surrender in World War II.

name: Modern Japan (Postwar)
note: Post-1947 Japan following American occupation and the 1947 Constitution. Parliamentary democracy with a figurehead emperor; economic miracle and rise as a technology and manufacturing superpower.

name: Modern Korea (Postwar)
note: Post-1945 Korea; note that North and South Korea diverged into separate states (Democratic People's Republic and Republic of Korea) in 1948, but are modeled here as a unified entry representing the shared cultural and historical continuity. Future modeling should split into separate regime entries for DPRK and ROK.

name: Joseon Dynasty
note: Founded by Yi Seong-gye; shifted from Buddhist to Neo-Confucian ideology. Known for its stable bureaucracy and the invention of Hangul.

figures.name: Sejong the Great
figures.significance: Commissioned the creation of Hangul; oversaw a scientific and cultural golden age.

figures.name: Yi Sun-sin
figures.significance: Admiral who saved Joseon from Japanese invasion (Imjin War) through naval brilliance and the turtle ship.

name: Yamato Period
note: The emergence of the Yamato imperial line and the introduction of Buddhism and Chinese characters. Includes the Kofun and Asuka sub-periods.

figures.name: Prince Shotoku
figures.significance: Authored the Seventeen-Article Constitution and promoted Buddhism.

name: Tokugawa Shogunate (Edo Period)
note: A period of isolation (Sakoku) and peace under the Tokugawa shoguns. Decentralized but tightly controlled feudalism.

policies.name: Sakoku Edict of 1635
policies.description: Closed the country to foreigners and forbade Japanese from traveling abroad.

figures.name: Tokugawa Ieyasu
figures.significance: Unified Japan after the Battle of Sekigahara (1600) and founded the Tokugawa Shogunate.

name: Empire of Japan (Meiji)
note: Rapid modernization (Meiji Restoration) and transformation into a world power. Dismantled the feudal system and expanded across East Asia.

policies.name: Meiji Restoration
policies.description: Restored imperial rule and abolished the shogunate.

figures.name: Emperor Meiji
figures.significance: Presided over the modernization and transformation of Japan.
